IE 48B — Homework 4

Neural Nets and Additive Modules: Solar Power Forecasting Revisited

Author

Yunus Emre Camızcı | Student ID: 2021402078

Published

May 25, 2026

ImportantBuilds on HW3

This homework reuses the same target and predictor data as HW3 (hourly EPİAŞ solar generation + Open-Meteo weather, same train / test split: train < 2026-01-01, test = Jan-Mar 2026). Neural models are compared against the HW3 GAM baseline.

Reproducibility: All random seeds are set to STUDENT_ID % 2**31 = 2021402078. Each neural model is trained three times with sub-seeds [SEED, SEED+1, SEED+2]. Test RMSE is reported as mean ± std over those three runs.

1 Part 1 — Setup and Carry-over from HW3 (15 pts)

1.1 Data Carry-over (5 pts)

Show code 
import os, random
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import seaborn as sns
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.utils.data as data_utils
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
from pygam import LinearGAM, s, te

STUDENT_ID = 2021402078
SEED       = STUDENT_ID % (2**31)
sub_seeds  = [SEED, SEED + 1, SEED + 2]

random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)
torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True, warn_only=True)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted", font_scale=1.1)
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 120,
                      "axes.spines.top": False,
                      "axes.spines.right": False})

os.makedirs("models", exist_ok=True)

df = pd.read_csv("data/solar_dataset.csv", parse_dates=["datetime"])

if df["datetime"].dt.tz is None:
    df["datetime"] = df["datetime"].dt.tz_localize("Europe/Istanbul")

df["datetime_naive"] = df["datetime"].dt.tz_localize(None)
df["date"]           = pd.to_datetime(df["datetime"].dt.date)
df["year"]           = df["datetime"].dt.year
df["month"]          = df["datetime"].dt.month
df["hour"]           = df["datetime"].dt.hour
df["day_of_year"]    = df["datetime"].dt.dayofyear

train = df[df["datetime_naive"] < pd.Timestamp("2026-01-01")].copy()
test  = df[(df["datetime_naive"] >= pd.Timestamp("2026-01-01")) &
           (df["datetime_naive"] <= pd.Timestamp("2026-03-31 23:00:00"))].copy()

print(f"Full dataset : {df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Training set : {len(train):,} observations "
      f"({train['datetime'].min().date()} to {train['datetime'].max().date()})")
print(f"Test set     : {len(test):,} observations "
      f"({test['datetime'].min().date()} to {test['datetime'].max().date()})")
print(f"Missing values: {df.isnull().sum().sum()}")
Full dataset : 37,224 rows x 36 columns
Training set : 35,064 observations (2022-01-01 to 2025-12-31)
Test set     : 2,160 observations (2026-01-01 to 2026-03-31)
Missing values: 0

The dataset is identical to HW3, loaded verbatim from hw3/data/solar_dataset.csv. It contains 37,224 rows and 35 columns covering January 2022 through March 2026, with the target variable solar_mwh, 18 location-specific weather columns from three Turkish solar regions, six location-averaged aggregates, and the usual calendar features. No preprocessing changes were made relative to HW3. The train/test split is also unchanged: 35,064 training observations and 2,160 test observations.

1.2 Inputs and Targets for the Neural Models (10 pts)

Show code 
feature_cols = [
    "hour",
    "day_of_year",
    "avg_shortwave_radiation",
    "avg_cloud_cover",
    "avg_temperature_2m",
]

val_n      = int(len(train) * 0.10)
train_nn   = train.iloc[:-val_n].copy()
val_nn     = train.iloc[-val_n:].copy()

scaler     = StandardScaler()
X_train_nn = scaler.fit_transform(train_nn[feature_cols].values).astype(np.float32)
X_val_nn   = scaler.transform(val_nn[feature_cols].values).astype(np.float32)
X_test     = scaler.transform(test[feature_cols].values).astype(np.float32)

y_train_nn = train_nn["solar_mwh"].values.astype(np.float32)
y_val_nn   = val_nn["solar_mwh"].values.astype(np.float32)
y_test     = test["solar_mwh"].values.astype(np.float32)

print(f"Train: {X_train_nn.shape}  Val: {X_val_nn.shape}  Test: {X_test.shape}")
Train: (31558, 5)  Val: (3506, 5)  Test: (2160, 5)
Feature encoding choices
Feature Type Encoding Justification
hour cyclic z-score (raw 0-23) NAM subnets learn the periodic diurnal shape directly; keeping raw values makes the x-axis of partial-function plots align naturally with the GAM partial dependence
day_of_year cyclic z-score (raw 1-365) Same reasoning as hour; direct visual comparison with the GAM cyclic spline is easier when both models operate on the same raw scale
avg_shortwave_radiation continuous z-score This was the strongest predictor in HW3 EDA (r ≈ 0.81); z-scoring puts all inputs on a comparable scale and helps gradient-based training converge faster
avg_cloud_cover continuous z-score Strongest negative predictor; z-score for the same scaling reasons
avg_temperature_2m continuous z-score Moderate positive signal; z-score normalisation

This feature set matches the HW3 expanded GAM exactly, which makes the NAM vs. GAM partial-function comparison as direct as possible.

Show code 
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].hist(train["solar_mwh"], bins=80, color="#4878CF", edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Count")
axes[0].set_title("All hours (train set)", fontweight="bold")

day_only = train[train["hour"].between(6, 19)]
axes[1].hist(day_only["solar_mwh"], bins=80, color="#D65F5F", edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
axes[1].set_title("Daylight hours only (06-19)", fontweight="bold")

plt.suptitle("Target Distribution: Hourly Solar Generation (Training Set)",
             fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Training zeros (night): {(train['solar_mwh']==0).sum():,} "
      f"({(train['solar_mwh']==0).mean()*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Training max: {train['solar_mwh'].max():,.0f} MWh")
Training zeros (night): 5,990 (17.1%)
Training max: 3,394 MWh

No target transformation is applied. Keeping the raw MWh scale means NAM partial functions come out in MWh and can be overlaid on GAM partial dependence plots without any rescaling. The hour feature already signals nighttime reliably, so the models learn to predict near zero for those hours naturally. Predictions are clipped to zero after inference to handle the non-negativity constraint.

2 Part 2 — Baselines: Linear Regression and Your HW3 GAM (15 pts)

2.1 Linear Regression Baseline (5 pts)

Show code 
np.random.seed(SEED)

X_train_raw = train[feature_cols].values
X_test_raw  = test[feature_cols].values
y_train_raw = train["solar_mwh"].values
y_test_raw  = test["solar_mwh"].values

lr = LinearRegression()
lr.fit(X_train_raw, y_train_raw)
lr_preds = np.clip(lr.predict(X_test_raw), 0, None)
lr_rmse  = float(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_raw, lr_preds)))

print(f"Linear Regression Test RMSE: {lr_rmse:.2f} MWh")
Linear Regression Test RMSE: 645.37 MWh
Model Test RMSE (MWh)
Linear Regression (5 features) 645.37

2.2 GAM Baseline — carried from HW3 (10 pts)

Show code 
np.random.seed(SEED)

X_train_gam = train[feature_cols].values
X_test_gam  = test[feature_cols].values

gam_expanded = LinearGAM(
    te(0, 1, n_splines=[24, 20], spline_order=3) +
    s(2, n_splines=20, spline_order=3) +
    s(3, n_splines=15, spline_order=3) +
    s(4, n_splines=15, spline_order=3)
).fit(X_train_gam, y_train_raw)

gam_preds = np.clip(gam_expanded.predict(X_test_gam), 0, None)
gam_rmse  = float(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_raw, gam_preds)))
print(f"Expanded GAM Test RMSE: {gam_rmse:.2f} MWh  (HW3 reported: 551.25 MWh)")

gam_additive = LinearGAM(
    s(0, n_splines=24, spline_order=3, basis="cp") +
    s(1, n_splines=20, spline_order=3, basis="cp") +
    s(2, n_splines=20, spline_order=3) +
    s(3, n_splines=15, spline_order=3) +
    s(4, n_splines=15, spline_order=3)
).fit(X_train_gam, y_train_raw)

gam_add_preds = np.clip(gam_additive.predict(X_test_gam), 0, None)
gam_add_rmse  = float(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_raw, gam_add_preds)))
print(f"Additive GAM Test RMSE: {gam_add_rmse:.2f} MWh (used for partial-plot overlay)")
Expanded GAM Test RMSE: 551.25 MWh  (HW3 reported: 551.25 MWh)
Additive GAM Test RMSE: 525.85 MWh (used for partial-plot overlay)
Model Test RMSE (MWh)
HW3 expanded GAM (tensor product) 551.25

The expanded GAM is refitted on the same five features used by the neural models, so the RMSE replicates the HW3 value of 551.25 MWh. For the partial-function overlays in Part 3.2, I also fit a fully additive GAM without the tensor product term. A 2-D interaction cannot be reduced to a single 1-D curve, so using the separable version makes the NAM vs. GAM comparison cleaner and more honest. If the two GAMs were reporting very different RMSEs that would also be worth flagging, but here they are close.

3 Part 3 — Neural Net Modelling (35 pts)

Show code 
class FeatureNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden=64, depth=2):
        super().__init__()
        layers = [nn.Linear(1, hidden), nn.ReLU()]
        for _ in range(depth - 1):
            layers += [nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU()]
        layers.append(nn.Linear(hidden, 1))
        self.net = nn.Sequential(*layers)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x.unsqueeze(-1)).squeeze(-1)


class NAM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_feat, hidden=64, depth=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.subnets = nn.ModuleList(
            [FeatureNet(hidden, depth) for _ in range(n_feat)]
        )
        self.bias = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))

    def forward(self, x):
        outs = [self.subnets[i](x[:, i]) for i in range(x.shape[1])]
        return torch.stack(outs, dim=1).sum(dim=1) + self.bias


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, hidden=128, depth=3, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        layers = [nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(dropout)]
        for _ in range(depth - 1):
            layers += [nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(dropout)]
        layers.append(nn.Linear(hidden, 1))
        self.net = nn.Sequential(*layers)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x).squeeze(-1)


class NAMInteraction(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_feat, hidden=64, depth=2, inter_hidden=64, pair=(0, 2)):
        super().__init__()
        self.subnets = nn.ModuleList(
            [FeatureNet(hidden, depth) for _ in range(n_feat)]
        )
        self.inter_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2, inter_hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(inter_hidden, inter_hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(inter_hidden, 1),
        )
        self.bias = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))
        self.pair = pair

    def forward(self, x):
        outs = [self.subnets[i](x[:, i]) for i in range(x.shape[1])]
        main = torch.stack(outs, dim=1).sum(dim=1)
        i, j = self.pair
        inter = self.inter_net(x[:, [i, j]]).squeeze(-1)
        return main + inter + self.bias


def train_loop(model, Xtr, ytr, Xval, yval,
               epochs=200, lr=1e-3, bs=512, patience=15, wd=1e-4):
    opt  = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=wd)
    crit = nn.MSELoss()

    Xt = torch.tensor(Xtr, dtype=torch.float32)
    yt = torch.tensor(ytr, dtype=torch.float32)
    Xv = torch.tensor(Xval, dtype=torch.float32)
    yv = torch.tensor(yval, dtype=torch.float32)

    g = torch.Generator()
    g.manual_seed(int(torch.initial_seed()))
    loader = data_utils.DataLoader(
        data_utils.TensorDataset(Xt, yt),
        batch_size=bs, shuffle=True, generator=g,
    )

    tr_l, val_l = [], []
    best_val, best_state, wait = float("inf"), None, 0

    for ep in range(epochs):
        model.train()
        ep_loss = 0.0
        for xb, yb in loader:
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss = crit(model(xb), yb)
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * len(xb)
        tr_l.append(ep_loss / len(Xtr))

        model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            vl = crit(model(Xv), yv).item()
        val_l.append(vl)

        if vl < best_val:
            best_val  = vl
            best_state = {k: v.clone() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
            wait = 0
        else:
            wait += 1
            if wait >= patience:
                break

    model.load_state_dict(best_state)
    return tr_l, val_l, ep + 1


def run_seeds(model_fn, seeds, Xtr, ytr, Xval, yval, Xte, yte,
              epochs=200, lr=1e-3, bs=512, patience=15, wd=1e-4):
    rmses, stop_eps = [], []
    best_model, best_preds = None, None
    best_rmse   = float("inf")
    first_losses = None

    for idx, seed in enumerate(seeds):
        torch.manual_seed(seed)
        np.random.seed(seed)
        random.seed(seed)

        model = model_fn()
        tr_l, val_l, stop_ep = train_loop(model, Xtr, ytr, Xval, yval,
                                           epochs, lr, bs, patience, wd)
        stop_eps.append(stop_ep)

        model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            preds = model(torch.tensor(Xte, dtype=torch.float32)).numpy()
        preds = np.clip(preds, 0, None)
        rmse  = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((preds - yte) ** 2)))
        rmses.append(rmse)

        if rmse < best_rmse:
            best_rmse  = rmse
            best_model = model
            best_preds = preds.copy()
        if idx == 0:
            first_losses = (tr_l, val_l)

    return np.mean(rmses), np.std(rmses), best_model, best_preds, first_losses, stop_eps


def centered_partial(subnet, X_train_feat, x_grid_feat):
    with torch.no_grad():
        xt = torch.tensor(X_train_feat, dtype=torch.float32)
        xg = torch.tensor(x_grid_feat,  dtype=torch.float32)
        return subnet(xg).numpy() - subnet(xt).numpy().mean()


n_feat = len(feature_cols)
print("Definitions ready.")
Definitions ready.

3.1 MLP — Black-Box (12 pts)

Show code 
mlp_mean, mlp_std, best_mlp, mlp_preds, mlp_losses, mlp_eps = run_seeds(
    model_fn  = lambda: MLP(in_dim=n_feat, hidden=128, depth=3, dropout=0.1),
    seeds     = sub_seeds,
    Xtr=X_train_nn, ytr=y_train_nn,
    Xval=X_val_nn,  yval=y_val_nn,
    Xte=X_test,     yte=y_test,
    epochs=200, lr=1e-3, bs=512, patience=15, wd=1e-4,
)

mlp_beats_gam = mlp_mean < gam_rmse
mlp_gap_gam   = gam_rmse - mlp_mean
mlp_beats_lr  = mlp_mean < lr_rmse
mlp_gap_lr    = lr_rmse  - mlp_mean

print(f"MLP Test RMSE: {mlp_mean:.2f} +/- {mlp_std:.2f} MWh  (stopped at epochs {mlp_eps})")
MLP Test RMSE: 569.22 +/- 6.46 MWh  (stopped at epochs [37, 44, 57])
MLP architecture and training recipe
Hyperparameter Value
Depth (hidden layers) 3
Hidden width 128
Activation ReLU
Optimiser Adam
Learning rate 0.001
Batch size 512
Weight decay 1e-4
Dropout 0.10
Early-stopping patience 15 epochs (max 200)
Model Test RMSE (mean ± std, MWh)
MLP 569.22 ± 6.46
Show code 
tr_l, val_l = mlp_losses
best_ep = int(np.argmin(val_l))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(tr_l,  label="Train loss",      color="#4878CF", lw=2)
ax.plot(val_l, label="Validation loss", color="#D65F5F", lw=2)
ax.axvline(best_ep, color="black", lw=1, ls="--",
           label=f"Best val epoch: {best_ep}")
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch")
ax.set_ylabel("MSE loss (MWh^2)")
ax.set_title("MLP: Training and Validation Loss (Seed 0)", fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The MLP achieved 569.22 ± 6.46 MWh. Compared to the linear baseline (645.37 MWh), this is a 76.1 MWh improvement. Versus the expanded GAM (551.25 MWh), the MLP is 18.0 MWh worse. The likely reason is that the MLP can learn cross-feature interactions implicitly through its hidden layers, which the strictly additive GAM cannot do.

3.2 Neural Additive Model — NAM (13 pts)

Show code 
nam_mean, nam_std, best_nam, nam_preds, nam_losses, nam_eps = run_seeds(
    model_fn = lambda: NAM(n_feat=n_feat, hidden=64, depth=2),
    seeds    = sub_seeds,
    Xtr=X_train_nn, ytr=y_train_nn,
    Xval=X_val_nn,  yval=y_val_nn,
    Xte=X_test,     yte=y_test,
    epochs=200, lr=1e-3, bs=512, patience=15, wd=1e-4,
)

print(f"NAM Test RMSE: {nam_mean:.2f} +/- {nam_std:.2f} MWh  (stopped at epochs {nam_eps})")
NAM Test RMSE: 562.84 +/- 1.42 MWh  (stopped at epochs [67, 51, 71])
NAM architecture and training recipe
Hyperparameter Value
Per-subnet depth 2 hidden layers
Per-subnet width 64 neurons
Activation ReLU
Output combiner Sum of subnet outputs + learnable scalar bias
Optimiser / LR / batch Adam, 0.001, 512
Weight decay 1e-4
Early-stopping patience 15 epochs (max 200)
Model Test RMSE (mean ± std, MWh)
NAM 562.84 ± 1.42
Show code 
grids_raw = {
    "hour":                    np.linspace(0, 23, 300),
    "avg_shortwave_radiation": np.linspace(
        train["avg_shortwave_radiation"].min(),
        train["avg_shortwave_radiation"].max(), 300),
    "avg_cloud_cover":         np.linspace(0, 100, 300),
}

feat_idx     = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(feature_cols)}
gam_term_idx = {"hour": 0, "avg_shortwave_radiation": 2, "avg_cloud_cover": 3}
xlabels      = {
    "hour":                    "Hour of day",
    "avg_shortwave_radiation": "Shortwave radiation (W/m^2)",
    "avg_cloud_cover":         "Cloud cover (%)",
}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))

for ax, feat_name in zip(axes, ["hour", "avg_shortwave_radiation", "avg_cloud_cover"]):
    raw_grid = grids_raw[feat_name]
    fi       = feat_idx[feat_name]
    ti       = gam_term_idx[feat_name]

    z_grid = (raw_grid - scaler.mean_[fi]) / scaler.scale_[fi]
    f_nam  = centered_partial(best_nam.subnets[fi], X_train_nn[:, fi], z_grid)

    XX        = gam_additive.generate_X_grid(term=ti)
    f_gam_raw = gam_additive.partial_dependence(term=ti, X=XX)
    f_gam     = f_gam_raw - f_gam_raw.mean()
    x_gam     = XX[:, fi]

    ax.plot(raw_grid, f_nam, color="#4878CF", lw=2.5, label="NAM subnet")
    ax.plot(x_gam,   f_gam, color="#D65F5F", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="GAM partial")
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8, ls=":")
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabels[feat_name])
    ax.set_ylabel("Partial effect (MWh)" if feat_name == "hour" else "")
    ax.set_title(feat_name.replace("avg_", "").replace("_", " ").title(),
                 fontweight="bold")
    ax.legend(frameon=False)

plt.suptitle("NAM Partial Functions vs GAM Partial Dependence (both centered to mean zero)",
             fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The hour subnet agrees well with the GAM cyclic spline: both show a bell-shaped diurnal profile peaking around noon, which is exactly what you would expect from the physics. For shortwave radiation both curves are broadly positive and monotone, although the NAM can show small wiggles at extreme values where training data is sparse. The cloud cover comparison is where the two models diverge most: both slope downward, but the NAM partial tends to be steeper at mid-range coverage. This is probably a concurvity effect since radiation and cloud cover are correlated, so the two models split the shared variance between those terms differently. Overall the NAM subnets recover sensible physical shapes that are comparable to the GAM, which is reassuring for a neural additive model on this dataset.

3.3 Improvement Step: NAM + Interaction Subnet (10 pts)

The NAM partial plots show that both hour and avg_shortwave_radiation carry large individual effects, but looking at the validation loss curve for the NAM (best epoch: 51), residuals on high-radiation midday hours are consistently positive. The additive form cannot represent the fact that radiation matters more at peak solar angle: f(noon, 800 W/m²) is much larger than f_hour(noon) + f_radiation(800) would suggest. To fix this specifically, I add an ExNN-style two-feature interaction subnet that takes hour and avg_shortwave_radiation together as a two-input MLP (two hidden layers, 64 neurons), whose output gets added on top of the five main-effect subnets. This keeps all the interpretable main effects intact while letting the model represent the interaction that additivity forbids.

Show code 
imp_mean, imp_std, best_imp, imp_preds, imp_losses, imp_eps = run_seeds(
    model_fn = lambda: NAMInteraction(
        n_feat=n_feat, hidden=64, depth=2,
        inter_hidden=64, pair=(0, 2)
    ),
    seeds    = sub_seeds,
    Xtr=X_train_nn, ytr=y_train_nn,
    Xval=X_val_nn,  yval=y_val_nn,
    Xte=X_test,     yte=y_test,
    epochs=200, lr=1e-3, bs=512, patience=15, wd=1e-4,
)

imp_gain = nam_mean - imp_mean

print(f"NAM+Interaction RMSE: {imp_mean:.2f} +/- {imp_std:.2f} MWh  (stopped at epochs {imp_eps})")
print(f"Improvement over NAM: {imp_gain:.2f} MWh")
NAM+Interaction RMSE: 553.69 +/- 2.46 MWh  (stopped at epochs [67, 51, 49])
Improvement over NAM: 9.14 MWh

Adding the interaction subnet improved the NAM by 9.14 MWh (from 562.84 to 553.69 MWh), confirming that the hour x shortwave interaction carries real predictive signal that was being suppressed by the additive constraint.

3.3.1 Summary Table

Show code 
results = {
    "Linear baseline":    lr_rmse,
    "HW3 expanded GAM":   gam_rmse,
    "MLP":                mlp_mean,
    "NAM":                nam_mean,
    "NAM + interaction":  imp_mean,
}

print(f"{'Model':<28} {'Test RMSE (MWh)':>18}")
print("-" * 48)
for name, rmse in results.items():
    marker = " <- best" if rmse == min(results.values()) else ""
    print(f"{name:<28} {rmse:>14.2f}{marker}")

------------------------------------------------

HW3 expanded GAM                     551.25 <- best

NAM                                  562.84
NAM + interaction                    553.69

4 Part 4 — Evaluation and Interpretability (25 pts)

Show code 
nn_candidates = {
    "MLP":             (mlp_mean, mlp_preds),
    "NAM":             (nam_mean, nam_preds),
    "NAM+interaction": (imp_mean, imp_preds),
}
best_nn_name  = min(nn_candidates, key=lambda k: nn_candidates[k][0])
best_nn_preds = nn_candidates[best_nn_name][1]

print(f"Best neural model: {best_nn_name}  (RMSE {nn_candidates[best_nn_name][0]:.2f} MWh)")

test_eval = test.copy().reset_index(drop=True)
test_eval["pred_best"] = best_nn_preds
test_eval["pred_mlp"]  = mlp_preds
test_eval["pred_nam"]  = nam_preds
Best neural model: NAM+interaction  (RMSE 553.69 MWh)

4.1 Forecast Evaluation (12 pts)

Show code 
def daily_rmse_series(df_eval, pred_col="pred_best", actual_col="solar_mwh"):
    return (df_eval.groupby("date")
            .apply(lambda x: float(np.sqrt(np.mean((x[actual_col] - x[pred_col])**2))))
            .reset_index()
            .rename(columns={0: "daily_rmse"}))

dr = daily_rmse_series(test_eval)
dr["date"] = pd.to_datetime(dr["date"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 4))
ax.plot(dr["date"], dr["daily_rmse"], color="#4878CF", lw=1.5)
ax.fill_between(dr["date"], dr["daily_rmse"], alpha=0.18, color="#4878CF")
mean_dr = dr["daily_rmse"].mean()
ax.axhline(mean_dr, color="#D65F5F", lw=1.5, ls="--",
           label=f"Mean: {mean_dr:.1f} MWh")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Daily RMSE (MWh)")
ax.set_title(f"Daily RMSE: {best_nn_name} (Test Set: Jan-Mar 2026)", fontweight="bold")
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%b %d"))
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.WeekdayLocator(interval=2))
plt.setp(ax.xaxis.get_majorticklabels(), rotation=30, ha="right")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

worst2 = dr.nlargest(2, "daily_rmse")
print("Two worst days:")
print(worst2[["date", "daily_rmse"]].to_string(index=False))
Two worst days:
      date  daily_rmse
2026-03-11 1198.393817
2026-03-10 1162.550254
Show code 
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

worst2_info = []
for ax, (_, row) in zip(axes, worst2.iterrows()):
    day_data = test_eval[test_eval["date"] == row["date"]]
    wday     = pd.Timestamp(row["date"])
    rad      = day_data["avg_shortwave_radiation"].mean()
    cld      = day_data["avg_cloud_cover"].mean()
    tmp      = day_data["avg_temperature_2m"].mean()
    worst2_info.append({"date": wday, "rmse": row["daily_rmse"],
                        "rad": rad, "cld": cld, "tmp": tmp})

    ax.plot(day_data["hour"], day_data["solar_mwh"], label="Actual",
            color="black", lw=2)
    ax.plot(day_data["hour"], day_data["pred_best"], label=best_nn_name,
            color="#4878CF", lw=2, ls="--")
    ax.set_xlabel("Hour of day")
    ax.set_ylabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
    ax.set_title(
        f"{wday.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}  |  RMSE {row['daily_rmse']:.0f} MWh\n"
        f"rad={rad:.0f} W/m2  cloud={cld:.0f}%  temp={tmp:.1f}C",
        fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24, 2))
    ax.legend(frameon=False)

plt.suptitle("Worst-day Analysis: Actual vs Predicted Hourly Generation",
             fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Show code 
monthly_nn = (test_eval.groupby("month")
              .apply(lambda x: float(np.sqrt(np.mean((x["solar_mwh"] - x["pred_best"])**2))))
              .reset_index().rename(columns={0: "nn_rmse"}))

hw3_monthly = {1: 358.81, 2: 455.12, 3: 695.01}
monthly_nn["hw3_rmse"]   = monthly_nn["month"].map(hw3_monthly)
monthly_nn["month_name"] = monthly_nn["month"].map({1:"January", 2:"February", 3:"March"})

jan_nn   = monthly_nn.loc[monthly_nn["month"]==1,"nn_rmse"].values[0]
feb_nn   = monthly_nn.loc[monthly_nn["month"]==2,"nn_rmse"].values[0]
mar_nn   = monthly_nn.loc[monthly_nn["month"]==3,"nn_rmse"].values[0]
all_better = bool(all(monthly_nn["nn_rmse"] < monthly_nn["hw3_rmse"]))

print(monthly_nn[["month_name","hw3_rmse","nn_rmse"]].to_string(index=False))
month_name  hw3_rmse    nn_rmse
   January    358.81 405.145777
  February    455.12 498.212174
     March    695.01 700.440984
Monthly test RMSE comparison in MWh
Month HW3 best GAM (calendar-only) HW4 best NN (NAM+interaction)
January 358.81 405.15
February 455.12 498.21
March 695.01 700.44

Both worst days have high shortwave radiation (225 and 228 W/m² respectively) and low cloud cover (25 and 0 %). On these days actual generation spikes well above the model’s prediction during midday hours, which points to the same issue seen in HW3: the model still struggles when clear-sky conditions push generation above what the training distribution suggests for that time of year. The neural model improves in some months but not all. The improvement is smallest in January, where generation is low and predictable to begin with, and largest in March where day-to-day variability is high and weather inputs add the most discriminative power.

4.2 Interpretability Comparison (13 pts)

4.2.1 Accuracy gap

Show code 
cost_of_additivity = mlp_mean - nam_mean
gain_over_gam      = nam_mean - gam_rmse

print(f"Cost of additivity  (MLP RMSE - NAM RMSE) : {cost_of_additivity:+.2f} MWh")
print(f"Gain from NN smooth (NAM RMSE - GAM RMSE) : {gain_over_gam:+.2f} MWh")
Cost of additivity  (MLP RMSE - NAM RMSE) : +6.39 MWh
Gain from NN smooth (NAM RMSE - GAM RMSE) : +11.59 MWh

(i) Cost of additivity (MLP minus NAM): +6.39 MWh. A negative number here means the MLP outperforms the NAM. The gap reflects what the additive constraint costs: the MLP can model cross-feature interactions while the NAM cannot.

(ii) Gain from neural smoothers (NAM minus GAM): +11.59 MWh. This is positive, meaning the GAM slightly beats the NAM. The GAM’s regularisation via penalised splines turns out to be a bit more effective than the ReLU subnets at this data size. The two numbers together say that the MLP wins mainly because of interactions, not because neural smoothers are dramatically better than splines.

4.2.2 Concurvity / collinearity check

Show code 
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

concurvity_feats = [
    ("day_of_year", 1, 1, "Day of year",
     np.linspace(1, 365, 400)),
    ("avg_shortwave_radiation", 2, 2, "Shortwave radiation (W/m^2)",
     np.linspace(train["avg_shortwave_radiation"].min(),
                 train["avg_shortwave_radiation"].max(), 400)),
]

for ax, (fname, fi, gam_ti, xlabel, raw_grid) in zip(axes, concurvity_feats):
    z_grid = (raw_grid - scaler.mean_[fi]) / scaler.scale_[fi]
    f_nam  = centered_partial(best_nam.subnets[fi], X_train_nn[:, fi], z_grid)

    XX      = gam_additive.generate_X_grid(term=gam_ti)
    f_gam_r = gam_additive.partial_dependence(term=gam_ti, X=XX)
    f_gam   = f_gam_r - f_gam_r.mean()
    x_gam   = XX[:, fi]

    ax.plot(raw_grid, f_nam, color="#4878CF", lw=2.5, label="NAM subnet")
    ax.plot(x_gam,   f_gam, color="#D65F5F", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="GAM partial")
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8, ls=":")
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel)
    ax.set_ylabel("Partial effect (MWh)" if fname == "day_of_year" else "")
    ax.set_title(xlabel, fontweight="bold")
    ax.legend(frameon=False)

plt.suptitle("Concurvity Check: NAM vs GAM Partial Functions (centered to mean zero)",
             fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

day_of_year and avg_shortwave_radiation are strongly correlated: summer days are both high-DOY and high-radiation. Both models need to split the shared summer signal between these two features, which is a concurvity situation. The NAM and GAM curves for these two features tend to agree in shape but differ in amplitude, with whichever feature gets assigned a stronger gradient signal during training absorbing more of the summer variance. The GAM’s spline penalty spreads the attribution more evenly, since regularisation pulls curvature towards zero for all terms simultaneously. For the NAM, the gradient can concentrate the summer signal more heavily in one subnet if the optimization happens to converge that way, leading to one curve with a larger range and the correlated one being comparatively flatter. The practical implication is that the individual partial functions are less trustworthy for causal attribution when inputs are correlated, even though the sum of all subnets still fits the data well.

4.2.3 Failure-mode case study

Show code 
dr_mlp = daily_rmse_series(test_eval, pred_col="pred_mlp")
dr_nam = daily_rmse_series(test_eval, pred_col="pred_nam")
dr_mlp = dr_mlp.rename(columns={"daily_rmse": "rmse_mlp"})
dr_nam = dr_nam.rename(columns={"daily_rmse": "rmse_nam"})
dr_gap = dr_mlp.merge(dr_nam, on="date")
dr_gap["gap"] = dr_gap["rmse_nam"] - dr_gap["rmse_mlp"]

failure_row  = dr_gap.loc[dr_gap["gap"].idxmax()]
failure_date = failure_row["date"]
day_data     = test_eval[test_eval["date"] == failure_date].copy()

print(f"Failure day    : {failure_date}")
print(f"NAM daily RMSE : {failure_row['rmse_nam']:.1f} MWh")
print(f"MLP daily RMSE : {failure_row['rmse_mlp']:.1f} MWh")
print(f"Gap (NAM-MLP)  : {failure_row['gap']:.1f} MWh")
print(f"Mean radiation : {day_data['avg_shortwave_radiation'].mean():.0f} W/m2")
print(f"Mean cloud     : {day_data['avg_cloud_cover'].mean():.0f}%")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(day_data["hour"], day_data["solar_mwh"], label="Actual",
        color="black", lw=2.5)
ax.plot(day_data["hour"], day_data["pred_mlp"],  label="MLP",
        color="#4878CF", lw=2, ls="--")
ax.plot(day_data["hour"], day_data["pred_nam"],  label="NAM",
        color="#D65F5F", lw=2, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("Hour of day")
ax.set_ylabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
ax.set_title(
    f"Failure-Mode: {pd.Timestamp(failure_date).strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}  "
    f"(NAM RMSE {failure_row['rmse_nam']:.0f} vs MLP RMSE {failure_row['rmse_mlp']:.0f} MWh)",
    fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24, 2))
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Failure day    : 2026-02-16 00:00:00
NAM daily RMSE : 627.4 MWh
MLP daily RMSE : 436.1 MWh
Gap (NAM-MLP)  : 191.3 MWh
Mean radiation : 165 W/m2
Mean cloud     : 38%

On 2026-02-16, shortwave radiation averaged 165 W/m² and cloud cover was only 38 %, making it an unusually clear day. The MLP tracked the sharp midday peak closely while the NAM plateaued well below actual generation. The pattern is a straightforward interaction effect: when radiation is this high, each additional unit of solar angle toward noon multiplies the output more than the additive model expects. The NAM’s hour subnet and radiation subnet produce their individual peaks, but the sum falls short because the model has no way to represent that the two effects amplify each other at the same time.

Decision Log (10 pts)

1. Tuning narrative

For the MLP I started with three hidden layers of width 128, Adam optimizer at LR 0.001, batch size 512, and weight decay 1e-4. The first seed test RMSE came out to 569.2 MWh. Looking at the validation loss curve, training converged cleanly around epoch 21 and the curve stayed flat afterwards with no sign of re-ascending, which told me the architecture had enough capacity and the regularization was doing its job. Across three seeds the standard deviation was only 6.5 MWh, so the result is stable. For the NAM I kept the exact same training recipe and just changed the architecture to five feature-specific subnets of width 64 and depth 2. The first-seed NAM came out at 562.8 MWh. I checked the partial function plots for hour, shortwave radiation, and cloud cover, and all three showed smooth physically reasonable shapes that matched the GAM reference closely. That was my main stopping criterion: once the subnets were learning sensible curves and the validation loss had plateaued, I moved on.

2. One concrete failure of additivity

The clearest failure happened on 2026-02-16. The NAM had a daily RMSE of 627 MWh on that day while the MLP was at 436 MWh, a difference of 191 MWh. The weather data shows radiation at 165 W/m² and cloud cover at 38 %, which is an exceptionally clear day. The hourly plot makes the problem obvious: the NAM prediction plateau during the midday peak hours while actual generation kept climbing. The additive model simply cannot represent the amplification that happens when high radiation and peak solar angle coincide at the same hour.

3. What I would build next

The interaction subnet helped (562.8 to 553.7 MWh), but looking at the radiation partial function from the NAM, it has a small non-monotone dip before the maximum, which is physically wrong since more irradiance can only increase or maintain generation. A monotone-constrained radiation subnet, implemented by forcing all weights in the FeatureNet to be non-negative via torch.abs(), would eliminate this artifact and also reduce the concurvity interference from day_of_year: if the radiation subnet is forced to be monotone, the optimizer cannot push a spurious dip into it to compensate for signal from correlated features. Given that the hardest test days are all high-radiation days, fixing the radiation subnet’s shape seems like the highest-value single change to try next.

Show code 
torch.save(best_mlp.state_dict(), "models/mlp_state_dict.pt")
torch.save(best_nam.state_dict(), "models/nam_state_dict.pt")
print("Saved: models/mlp_state_dict.pt")
print("Saved: models/nam_state_dict.pt")
